items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.

items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: What does a mole of a substance represent?
items.correct: Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23) of particles of that substance.
items.misconception: A mole is a unit of mass equal to the relative molecular mass in grams of any substance.
items.random_wrong: A mole is a unit of volume equal to 24 dm^3 at any temperature.
items.why: Students conflate the mole (a count) with molar mass (a mass-per-mole), because in practice they use moles to convert to grams.

items.q: Increasing pressure in N2 + 3H2 to 2NH3 shifts equilibrium:
items.why: It's right (products). “left (reactants)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If forward reaction is exothermic, raising temperature shifts equilibrium:
items.why: It's left. “right” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: 36 cm^3 H2 produced in 60 s. Mean rate in cm^3/s?
items.why: The real figure is 0.6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
